title: Reversible nanoscale patterning of WTe$_2$ with a scanning tunneling microscope
authors: Kevin Hauser, Danyang Liu, Berk Zengin, Jens Oppliger, Samuel Mañas-Valero, Catherine Witteveen, Fabian O. von Rohr, Jennifer E. Hoffman, Fabian D. Natterer
abstract: Manipulating the lattice structure of ferroelectric quantum materials enables their use in low-power electronic devices, including field-effect transistors. WTe$_2$ is a Weyl-semimetal candidate and ferroelectric, both properties arising from the reduced crystal symmetry of its T$_\mathrm{d}$ ground state. The T$_\mathrm{d}$ crystal phase results from a Peierls distortion of the 1T parent structure and an interlayer shift. While experiments in WTe$_2$ have established ferroelectric switching and transient control of the predicted topological phase via ultrafast excitations, persistent electronic changes on the nanometer scale remain elusive. Here, we demonstrate that current pulses applied via scanning tunneling microscopy can both write and erase persistent nanometer-scale patterns on the surface of WTe$_2$. These patterns consist of apparent picometer in-plane and out-of-plane atomic displacements, accompanied by changes to the local density of states. The out-of-plane displacements further modulate the Peierls-like distortion present in WTe$_2$, while the in-plane displacements are indicative of ferroelectric switching. The induced patterns can be repositioned and erased, suggesting a nanoscale handle on the ferroelectric properties of WTe$_2$.
summary: This work reports on the successful demonstration of reversible, nanoscale patterning on WTe2 using scanning tunneling microscopy. By applying controlled current pulses, the researchers induced measurable atomic displacements and altered the local electronic structure. This technique provides a powerful, direct handle for manipulating the ferroelectric and topological properties of 2D quantum materials.
main_problem: The goal is to achieve persistent, reversible, nanoscale control over the electronic and structural phases of ferroelectric or Weyl semimetal quantum materials for use in low-power electronics.
main_result: The authors demonstrated that applying current pulses via STM can write and erase persistent nanometer-scale patterns on WTe2, inducing measurable atomic displacements and changes in the local density of states.
method: The experiment utilizes Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM) in a bias spectroscopy mode, applying controlled current pulses to induce localized structural and electronic modifications.
model_or_system: The physical system is Tungsten ditelluride (WTe2), a layered van der Waals material exhibiting both ferroelectric and Weyl semimetal candidate properties due to its Td crystal phase.
key_observables: Apparent picometer in-plane and out-of-plane atomic displacements, changes in the Local Density of States (LDOS), and the ability to observe distinct structural vs. electronic height signatures.
important_parameters: The patterning process requires high currents and electric fields, with displacements observed ($\sim\pm 50 	ext{ pm}$) being comparable to predicted ferroelectric switching shifts.
main_assumptions: The study assumes that the observed structural and electronic changes are directly controllable by the applied current pulses, and it notes that the available energy resolution limits precise measurement near the Weyl point energy.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the basic structure and induced patterns, LDOS spectra showing depletion/enhancement, and detailed maps showing apparent in-plane displacements along crystal axes ($\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$), alongside parameter maps guiding the patterning process.
paper_structure: The paper details the material synthesis methods, establishes the STM/STS measurement parameters, presents the experimental procedure using current pulses for writing/erasing, and analyzes the resulting structural and electronic signatures.
why_interesting: The ability to write and erase quantum material properties at the nanoscale using electrical pulses provides a direct, controllable platform for studying emergent quantum phenomena, which is highly relevant for developing quantum devices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Basic appearance of pattern. (a) Top and side view of WTe2 crystal structure. Unit cell is shown in red. We depict Te atoms at different heights in shades of yellow. The top view shows the W and Te atoms of the surface WTe2 layer. App illustrates the height difference between the upper and lower Te atoms at the surface. (b) Large topography showing both an unperturbed area and regions with patterns of various sizes. Recorded at a sample bias Vs =300 mV and set current Is =500 pA. The white dashed box indicates the region under analysis in Fig. 3. (c) and (d) show zoom-ins of the pristine WTe2 surface and the smallest unit of the created pattern, respectively. Locations are indicated...

FIG. 2. Local density of states of the patterned WTe2 surface. (a) Waterfall plot of the measured differential conductance g(r, E). The blue shaded spectra indicate the location of the patterns determined by a depletion in LDOS at −400 meV (Details in Appendix D). The directionality is indicated by the black triangle and square on the right side, as well as in (b) and (e). (b) Location of spectra shown in (a). Blue segments correspond to the ones in (a). Topography was recorded with Vs =300 mV and Is =2 nA. (c) Spatially averaged conductance ⟨g⟩r(E) of the patterned and unperturbed regions highlighted by the dashed boxes in (e). (d) Difference in conductance between LDOS spectra in (c). (e)...

FIG. 3. Spatial lock-in analysis to resolve apparent lattice distortions. (a) Topography corresponding to white dashed rectangle in Fig. 1(c). The four tire tracks of the base patterns are marked by purple circles to provide a spatial reference between panels (a) to (d). Lattice directions ⃗a and ⃗b are indicated in the top left corner. (b) and (c) show apparent in-plane displacements along ⃗a and ⃗b, extracted via spatial lock-in analysis. The plotted displacement values are obtained from the spatial lock-in phase map by rescaling with the unit cell length of the respective lattice direction. Arrows indicate displacement direction. (d) Apparent variation in Te-height corrugation App,...

FIG. 4. Base pattern movement and deletion. (a) to (c) show constant-current topographies recorded after write (a), move (b), and erase (c) operations (green, blue, and orange arrows explained in the legend on the right). The blue cross and green dashed line in (a) indicate the location of the spectra used for pattern creation and movement. The orange dashed rectangle in (b) indicates the location of the erase operation. Grid lines are included in (a) and (b) to illustrate the movement of the pattern along the Te row. The bright white dots on the top right are surface contaminants that act as positional reference between topographies. Lattice directions ⃗a and ⃗b are indicated in panel (c)....

FIG. 5. Patterning-parameter map with the tip-sample bias Vs shown on the x-axis instead of the calculated electric field E (Fig. 1(f)). Blue-shaded region serves as guide to the eye where patterning is possible. The green-shaded region high- lights STM setpoint values used in previous studies.8,25,27,38
